commit_messages: eafcffa: [Claude] Add 87 tests for v1.1.0 modules + bump build version
815511d: [Claude] Update COLLAB.md — tests complete, build verified
b80ddfa: [Claude] Security hardening: --no-config flag, rule suppression warning

findings.title: Phantom rule names in _SECURITY_RULES — 3 rules don't exist
findings.detail: _SECURITY_RULES contains 'command-injection', 'insecure-deserialization', and 'xss', but none of these rule names exist in languages.py, compliance.py, detector.py, or anywhere else in the codebase. They are phantom rules. If a malicious .doji.toml suppressed 'os-system' (real) and 'command-injection' (phantom), the warning would say '2 security rule(s)' when only 1 real rule is suppressed. The set gives a false sense of coverage — it looks like command injection, XSS, and insecure deserialization are recognized categories when they have no corresponding detection rules.
findings.evidence:
"command-injection", "path-traversal", "insecure-crypto",
"insecure-deserialization", "insecure-http", "xss",

Grep for these 3 names in dojigiri/ returns matches ONLY in __main__.py:184-185.
findings.suggestion: Remove the 3 phantom names. Derive the set from CWE_MAP keys to stay in sync automatically. Or add a test that asserts every entry in _SECURITY_RULES corresponds to an actual rule.

findings.title: AnthropicBackend.chat blindly indexes response.content[0] without guard
findings.detail: OpenAI backend got an empty-choices guard in commit 792b46a (immediately before this review range), but AnthropicBackend.chat still does `response.content[0].text` without checking if content is empty. The Anthropic API can return an empty content array in edge cases (max_tokens=0, safety filter). This would crash with IndexError instead of a clear error.
findings.suggestion: Add: `if not response.content: raise RuntimeError('Anthropic returned empty response')` — mirrors the guard already in OpenAICompatibleBackend.

findings.title: test_chat_wraps_anthropic_sdk is mock theater — proves mock returns what was set up
findings.detail: The test sets up a mock to return 'response text' with 100/50 tokens, calls chat(), then asserts it got 'response text' with 100/50 tokens. If chat() were `return LLMResponse(text='response text', input_tokens=100, output_tokens=50)`, the test still passes. It doesn't verify the SDK was called with the correct model, temperature, max_tokens, or system prompt. Compare with test_chat_builds_correct_payload (line 108) for the OpenAI backend, which does verify all arguments — the Anthropic test should match that rigor.
findings.suggestion: Use assert_called_once_with() to verify model, max_tokens, temperature, system, and messages are forwarded correctly.

findings.title: No test coverage for --no-config flag or security rule suppression warning
findings.detail: The --no-config flag and security suppression warning are the entire substantive change in commit b80ddfa, but there are zero tests for either. No test verifies --no-config skips config loading. No test verifies the warning fires when security rules are suppressed. No test verifies it stays silent for non-security rules. This is the one security-hardening feature in the commit range — it should be tested.
findings.evidence: Grep for 'no.config' or 'no_config' in tests/ returns no matches.
findings.suggestion: Add tests: (1) --no-config results in empty project_config and custom_rules, (2) suppressing 'eval-usage' in config triggers stderr warning containing 'security rule', (3) suppressing 'todo-marker' does NOT trigger the warning.

findings.title: Empty-choices guard tests use unrealistic empty messages list
findings.detail: test_chat_empty_choices_raises and test_chat_missing_choices_raises both pass messages=[] to chat(). This means the request payload itself is malformed — no real caller would send zero messages. The test validates the guard from commit 792b46a but with inputs that would never occur in practice. A real scenario is: user sends a valid prompt, but the API returns empty choices due to content filtering or rate limiting.
findings.evidence: b.chat(system="sys", messages=[])  # testing empty response with empty request
findings.suggestion: Use messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "test"}] to simulate a realistic request that receives an unexpected empty-choices response.

findings.title: Taka audit committed to public repo contains workstation fingerprint
findings.detail: The audit JSON contains specific PIDs, port bindings, installed software (RemoteMouse, Spotify), Windows patch history with dates (KB5077181, 2026-02-12), and network service inventory. The repo remote is https://github.com/Inklling/Genesis (per the audit). If pushed, this is information disclosure — an attacker learns exact software inventory, patch level (21 days behind), and network exposure of the development machine.
findings.evidence:
"RemoteMouse (PID 7656) is listening on 0.0.0.0:1978"
"Most recent hotfix: KB5077181 installed 2026-02-12"
findings.suggestion: Either add reviews/*-taka-*.json to .gitignore, redact system-specific details, or keep audit results in a private location outside the repo.

findings.title: _SECURITY_RULES rebuilt on every scan invocation
findings.detail: The set literal is reconstructed every time cmd_scan() is called. It's a constant — should be module-level. Defining it inside the function also makes it invisible to tests and imports.
findings.suggestion: Move to module level as a frozenset. Makes it importable for testing and avoids per-call overhead.

findings.title: Nested patch.dict pattern is confusing — outer patch is a no-op
findings.detail: test_no_api_key_raises wraps patch.dict(clear=True) around a second patch.dict(clear=True) that manually filters ANTHROPIC_API_KEY. The outer one is immediately overridden. Same pattern in test_default_host (line 248) and test_default_model (line 275).
findings.suggestion: Single `patch.dict(os.environ, {}, clear=True)` achieves the same result. The inner reconstruction is redundant because clear=True already removes everything.

findings.title: Hardcoded version string will break on next version bump
findings.detail: test_version_footer asserts 'Dojigiri v1.1.0' — this must be manually updated with every release.
findings.suggestion: Import __version__ from dojigiri and assert f'Dojigiri v{__version__}' instead.

findings.title: Is 100% CWE coverage for all rules an intentional policy?
findings.detail: test_all_language_rules_have_cwe asserts every rule from list_all_rules() has a CWE mapping. Adding any new language rule without a CWE mapping will fail this test. Some rules (style, formatting) have tenuous CWE connections. Is this deliberate policy or will it become friction?
findings.evidence:
unmapped = [r["name"] for r in rules if r["name"] not in CWE_MAP]
assert unmapped == []
findings.suggestion: If intentional (compliance requirement), document the policy in a comment. If pragmatic, consider an explicit opt-out set for rules where CWE mapping is not meaningful.

verdict.summary: 3 phantom rules in security allowlist, missing empty-content guard on Anthropic backend, no tests for the core security hardening change, and Taka audit leaks workstation fingerprint to public repo.
